steps.title: Identify the cause-and-effect signal
steps.content: The passage states Rogerson's 2015 calculation was more accurate, then immediately introduces the reason—the 1920 calculation used only cardboard and string. The transition must link the outcome to its cause.

steps.title: Match the transition to the choices
steps.content:
The logical relationship is cause-effect — the 1920 calculation was less accurate because it used only cardboard and string. The blank needs a transition signaling that explanation.
  • Choice A — "Likewise,": signals similarity/addition, so fails — the two calculations are being contrasted, not equated. ✗
  • Choice B — "To that end,": signals purpose (done in order to achieve X), so fails — no goal-directed action is described. ✗
  • Choice C — "Of course,": signals an unsurprising explanation — naturally the older method was less accurate given its crude materials, so fits. ✓
  • Choice D — "Ultimately,": signals a final conclusion or outcome, so fails — the sentence explains a reason, not an end result. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
